skill.name: azure-cloud-migrate
skill.description: Assess AWS Lambda and other cloud workloads, then guide migration to Azure Functions with reports, mappings, and runtime-specific patterns.
skill.summary: Plan and guide cross-cloud migration from AWS Lambda workloads to Azure Functions.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, credential, URL, and script indicators, but review found them in Markdown guidance and code examples. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden executable payload, or malicious intent was found. The skill has low operational risk because it guides file creation and uses Azure documentation tools during migration.

low_findings.title: Operational Filesystem Writes Are Expected
low_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to create a migrated output directory and status file. This is expected migration behavior and is constrained to a new output path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem activity is explicitly documented as output generation, and the source directory is protected from modification. Human review should ensure generated files stay inside the requested workspace.

low_findings.title: External Tool And Documentation Use
low_findings.description: The skill asks assistants to consult Azure MCP best-practice and documentation tools. This is appropriate for cloud migration but creates normal network and tool-use exposure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced tools are named Azure documentation and best-practice helpers. No evidence found that these calls send secrets or user data to unauthorized destinations.

low_findings.title: Credential References Are Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: Azure credential variables are shown as managed identity configuration examples. They do not read or exfiltrate local credentials from the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples point users toward managed identity and away from API keys. They are static documentation snippets, not executable credential collection logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Examples Misclassified As Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Most command findings come from inline code, code fences, and Azure Functions snippets in Markdown reference files. These snippets are guidance, not executable installer logic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns are inside Markdown documentation and require user-driven implementation. No package script or automatic command runner was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure Runtime Attributes Misclassified As Dynamic Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: C# and Java Azure Functions examples use normal function annotations and attributes. Static analysis treated the word Function as a script constructor pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is Azure Functions reference material, not JavaScript dynamic code construction. The examples do not execute inside the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation URLs And Local Paths Are Benign References
dangerous_patterns.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft or Azure reference material, and relative paths are Markdown links between local reference files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are documentation links, and the relative paths are internal Markdown navigation. No evidence found of outbound exfiltration endpoints.

content.user_title: Migrate Cloud Workloads to Azure Functions
content.value_statement: Cloud migrations need accurate service mapping before teams rewrite code. This skill guides assessment, reports, and Azure Functions migration patterns.
actual_capabilities: Creates a migration assessment report before code changes.
Maps AWS Lambda services to Azure Functions equivalents.
Provides runtime-specific Azure Functions patterns for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, C#, Java, and PowerShell.
Guides binding-first migrations for storage, queues, events, databases, and timers.
Documents managed identity, RBAC, and Azure monitoring migration guidance.
Hands off assessment context to Azure preparation, validation, and deployment skills.
limitations: The primary detailed scenario is AWS Lambda to Azure Functions.
It does not deploy infrastructure by itself.
It requires user review before destructive actions or production deployment.
Generated migration output still needs local testing and cloud validation.

use_cases.title: Assess Lambda Migration Readiness
use_cases.description: Inventory AWS Lambda functions, map dependencies, and produce a readiness report for Azure Functions migration.

use_cases.title: Convert Serverless Function Patterns
use_cases.description: Translate Lambda handlers, triggers, environment settings, and bindings into Azure Functions programming models.

use_cases.title: Plan Secure Azure Deployment
use_cases.description: Prepare identity-first configuration, RBAC needs, monitoring mappings, and handoff inputs for Azure infrastructure generation.

prompt_templates.title: Assess a Lambda Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this AWS Lambda project for migration to Azure Functions. Create the required assessment report and list readiness risks.

prompt_templates.title: Map Services to Azure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Lambda triggers, IAM policies, and dependencies. Map each AWS service to an Azure equivalent with migration complexity.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate Function Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the assessment report to convert the Lambda functions to Azure Functions. Use bindings first and keep output in a new directory.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Handoff for Infrastructure
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize the migrated application, required Azure resources, identity model, RBAC roles, and deployment risks for azure-prepare.

output_examples.input: A repository with three Node.js Lambda handlers, S3 triggers, and DynamoDB access.
output: A migration assessment report with function inventory and service mapping.
A target Azure Functions project plan using blob triggers and Cosmos DB guidance.
A list of identity, RBAC, monitoring, and deployment follow-up items.

output_examples.input: A Python Lambda function that processes queue messages and writes results to storage.
output: A recommended Azure Functions Python v2 structure.
Trigger and binding choices for queue input and storage output.
Configuration notes for managed identity and app settings.

output_examples.input: A team needs to decide whether to migrate a serverless workload this sprint.
output: A readiness score with effort estimates.
Known migration blockers and compatibility notes.
Next steps for testing, infrastructure preparation, and deployment review.

best_practices: Run assessment before requesting code migration.
Keep generated Azure output separate from the source cloud project.
Use managed identity and Azure bindings wherever supported.
anti_patterns: Do not skip the assessment report and start rewriting functions immediately.
Do not hand-author function.json for runtimes that generate bindings metadata.
Do not deploy or delete resources without explicit user confirmation.

faq.question: What migration path does this skill cover best?
faq.answer: It provides the most detailed guidance for AWS Lambda migration to Azure Functions.

faq.question: Can it migrate code automatically?
faq.answer: It guides code conversion and project structure generation, but users must review and test all output.

faq.question: Does it deploy Azure infrastructure?
faq.answer: No. It hands off deployment preparation to Azure preparation, validation, and deployment workflows.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: It includes patterns for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, C#, Java, and PowerShell Azure Functions.

faq.question: How does it handle credentials?
faq.answer: It recommends managed identity, RBAC, and app settings instead of hardcoded secrets or connection strings.

faq.question: Is it safe for production workloads?
faq.answer: Use it for planning and migration assistance. Production changes still require explicit approval, testing, and deployment validation.
